articles.title: About this site
articles.contents:
This website is a collection of data visualizations created for MATH 230. The goal of this portfolio is to present data not only as numbers, but as stories that can be explored visually. Each page highlights a different part of my work, from earlier visualizations made during the semester to a more developed data story using a real-world dataset.
Data visualization is important because it helps make patterns, relationships, and comparisons easier to understand. A strong visualization should be both informative and visually engaging. Throughout this website, I use graphs, colors, layout, and written explanations to guide viewers through the meaning behind the data.
Past Data Visualizations
The first page displays selected visualizations from earlier projects in the semester. These include generative artwork, a plot from a ggplot extension project, an introductory statistics dashboard, a hand-drawn data postcard, and other visualizations created using R. Each piece includes a placard that explains the title, dataset, variables, and main message of the visualization.
Main Data Exhibit
The second page focuses on a real-world dataset about sleep health and daily performance. This exhibit explores how daily habits, work patterns, caffeine use, sleep aids, and sleep quality relate to health risk and cognitive performance.
The story begins with basic summaries of sleep duration and sleep quality. It then moves into relationships between daily habits and sleep outcomes, followed by stimulant use and sleep-related behaviors. Finally, the exhibit examines how sleep connects to health risk and performance.
Design Choices
For the main exhibit, I created a night-inspired color palette using deep blues, soft lavender, moonlight gray, and warm coral. These colors were chosen to reflect the theme of sleep while keeping the visualizations readable and consistent. The repeated palette helps the page feel like a cohesive virtual art exhibit rather than a collection of unrelated graphs.
Tools Used
This website was created using R Markdown and Distill. The visualizations were made primarily with ggplot2, along with other R tools for data cleaning, layout, and presentation. The project combines statistical thinking, visual design, and written explanation to communicate the story behind the data.
Reflection
Through this project, I learned that data visualization is not only about making graphs, but also about making thoughtful choices. Choosing variables, colors, graph types, labels, and explanations all affects how viewers understand the data. My goal was to create a website that feels organized, visually engaging, and meaningful while clearly communicating the patterns found in the data.

articles.title: create_infographic
articles.contents:
MATH 230 Website


Home
About
Past Data Visualizations
Story Boarding
☰


  
    
      
        
        
        
      
      
    
    
      
  Home


  About


  Past Data Visualizations


  Story Boarding

      
  






create_infographic




library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.2.1     ✔ readr     2.2.0
## ✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
## ✔ ggplot2   4.0.3     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.5     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
## ✔ purrr     1.2.2     
## ── Conflicts ──────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)

sleep <- read.csv("sleep_health_dataset.csv")

sleep_palette <- c(
  "midnight" = "#1B263B",
  "deep_blue" = "#415A77",
  "soft_blue" = "#778DA9",
  "moonlight" = "#E0E1DD",
  "lavender" = "#CDB4DB",
  "dream_pink" = "#FFC8DD",
  "sunrise_coral" = "#FFAF87"
)

sleep <- sleep %>%
  mutate(
    exercise_day = factor(exercise_day, labels = c("No Exercise", "Exercise")),
    felt_rested = factor(felt_rested, labels = c("Did Not Feel Rested", "Felt Rested")),
    sleep_aid_used = factor(sleep_aid_used, labels = c("No Sleep Aid", "Used Sleep Aid")),
    shift_work = factor(shift_work, labels = c("No Shift Work", "Shift Work"))
  )

# Summary statistics
avg_sleep <- round(mean(sleep$sleep_duration_hrs, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
avg_quality <- round(mean(sleep$sleep_quality_score, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
rested_pct <- round(mean(sleep$felt_rested == "Felt Rested", na.rm = TRUE) * 100, 1)

# A reusable minimal theme
theme_sleep <- function() {
  theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16, color = sleep_palette["midnight"]),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, color = sleep_palette["deep_blue"]),
      axis.title = element_text(color = sleep_palette["midnight"], face = "bold"),
      axis.text = element_text(color = sleep_palette["deep_blue"]),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "gray90")
    )
}

# Title block
title_plot <- ggplot() +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0.72,
           label = "The Architecture of Sleep",
           size = 10, fontface = "bold", color = sleep_palette["midnight"]) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0.45,
           label = "How Daily Habits Shape Rest and Performance",
           size = 5, color = sleep_palette["deep_blue"]) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0.18,
           label = "A visual exhibit exploring sleep duration, work habits, caffeine use, and health risk.",
           size = 4, color = sleep_palette["deep_blue"]) +
  xlim(0, 1) + ylim(0, 1) +
  theme_void()

# Stat cards
stat1 <- ggplot() +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0, xmax = 1, ymin = 0, ymax = 1, fill = sleep_palette["moonlight"], color = NA) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0.65, label = avg_sleep, size = 10, fontface = "bold", color = sleep_palette["midnight"]) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0.32, label = "Average Sleep Hours", size = 4.5, color = sleep_palette["deep_blue"]) +
  xlim(0, 1) + ylim(0, 1) +
  theme_void()

stat2 <- ggplot() +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0, xmax = 1, ymin = 0, ymax = 1, fill = sleep_palette["lavender"], color = NA) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0.65, label = avg_quality, size = 10, fontface = "bold", color = sleep_palette["midnight"]) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0.32, label = "Average Sleep Quality", size = 4.5, color = sleep_palette["deep_blue"]) +
  xlim(0, 1) + ylim(0, 1) +
  theme_void()

stat3 <- ggplot() +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0, xmax = 1, ymin = 0, ymax = 1, fill = sleep_palette["dream_pink"], color = NA) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0.65, label = paste0(rested_pct, "%"), size = 10, fontface = "bold", color = sleep_palette["midnight"]) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0.32, label = "Felt Rested", size = 4.5, color = sleep_palette["deep_blue"]) +
  xlim(0, 1) + ylim(0, 1) +
  theme_void()

# Teaser visualization 1
p1 <- ggplot(sleep, aes(x = sleep_duration_hrs, y = sleep_quality_score)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.08, color = sleep_palette["soft_blue"]) +
  geom_smooth(color = sleep_palette["sunrise_coral"], se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(
    title = "Sleep Duration and Sleep Quality",
    x = "Sleep Duration (hours)",
    y = "Sleep Quality Score"
  ) +
  theme_sleep()

# Teaser visualization 2
p2 <- ggplot(sleep, aes(x = work_hours_that_day, y = sleep_duration_hrs)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.08, color = sleep_palette["lavender"]) +
  geom_smooth(color = sleep_palette["midnight"], se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(
    title = "Work Hours and Sleep Duration",
    x = "Work Hours",
    y = "Sleep Duration (hours)"
  ) +
  theme_sleep()

# Teaser visualization 3
p3 <- ggplot(sleep, aes(x = sleep_disorder_risk, y = sleep_quality_score, fill = sleep_disorder_risk)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.9, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Healthy" = sleep_palette["soft_blue"],
    "Low" = sleep_palette["moonlight"],
    "Moderate" = sleep_palette["lavender"],
    "High" = sleep_palette["sunrise_coral"]
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Sleep Disorder Risk and Sleep Quality",
    x = "Sleep Disorder Risk",
    y = "Sleep Quality Score"
  ) +
  theme_sleep()

# Footer / call to action
footer_plot <- ggplot() +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0, xmax = 1, ymin = 0, ymax = 1, fill = sleep_palette["midnight"], color = NA) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0.62,
           label = "Explore the full exhibit to see how daily habits shape sleep, health, and performance.",
           size = 4.5, color = "white", fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0.28,
           label = "MATH 230 Data Visualization Exhibit",
           size = 4, color = sleep_palette["moonlight"]) +
  xlim(0, 1) + ylim(0, 1) +
  theme_void()

# Combine into infographic
infographic <- title_plot /
  (stat1 | stat2 | stat3) /
  (p1 | p2 | p3) /
  footer_plot +
  plot_layout(heights = c(1.1, 0.9, 2.3, 0.8))

infographic
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'gam' and formula = 'y ~ s(x, bs =
## "cs")'
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'gam' and formula = 'y ~ s(x, bs =
## "cs")'
## Warning: No shared levels found between `names(values)` of the manual
## scale and the data's fill values.
## No shared levels found between `names(values)` of the manual
## scale and the data's fill values.

# Save as image
ggsave("sleep_infographic.png", infographic, width = 14, height = 16, dpi = 300)
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'gam' and formula = 'y ~ s(x, bs =
## "cs")'
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'gam' and formula = 'y ~ s(x, bs =
## "cs")'
## Warning: No shared levels found between `names(values)` of the manual
## scale and the data's fill values.
## No shared levels found between `names(values)` of the manual
## scale and the data's fill values.

articles.title: MATH 230 Project 5 Website
articles.description: Welcome to the website. I hope you enjoy it!
articles.contents:
Welcome to My MATH 230 Data Visualization Portfolio
This website is a collection of visual work created throughout the semester in MATH 230. The projects on this site explore how data can be transformed into visual stories through color, structure, creativity, and statistical thinking.
The portfolio includes earlier visualizations from the semester, including generative artwork, a ggplot extension project, an introductory statistics dashboard, and a hand-drawn data postcard. It also features a larger virtual art exhibit built around a real-world dataset.



Featured Exhibit: The Architecture of Sleep
The Architecture of Sleep is a virtual data art exhibit about sleep health, daily habits, and performance. Using a real-world dataset from Kaggle, this exhibit explores how sleep duration, work hours, caffeine use, sleep aids, health risk, and cognitive performance are connected.
The exhibit begins by introducing the overall distribution of sleep duration and sleep quality. It then moves into a deeper story about how daily routines and stimulants may shape rest. Finally, it connects sleep patterns to health risk and cognitive performance.
The infographic below serves as an advertisement for the exhibit. It uses the same night-inspired color palette as the full project, with deep blues, soft lavender, moonlight gray, and warm coral. These colors help create a calm visual identity that reflects the theme of sleep while keeping the graphs readable and engaging.

articles.title: Past Data Visualizations
articles.description: This page displays selected visualizations from my work in MATH 230 this semester.
articles.contents:
Generative Art Project





Title: Artwork 1




Title: Artwork 2




Title: Artwork 3


ggplot() Extenstion Project



Title: Alluvial Plot of Sex vs. Average Time
Dataset and variables:
This visualization uses the Ninja dataset. The main variables are Sex, which groups observations by sex, and Time.new, which represents average time categories.
What the visualization shows:
This alluvial plot shows how observations flow from sex categories into average time categories. The widths of the flows represent how many observations fall into each combination of Sex and Time.new.
Introductory Statistics Visualization



Title: Lower-Tailed Randomization Test for Population Proportion
Dataset and variables:
This visualization is based on a simulated randomization distribution generated using a binomial model with sample size \(n = 100\) and null proportion \(p_0 = 0.30\). The key variable is the sample proportion (randomization.proportion), with a user-controlled observed proportion (xbar) adjusted via a slider.
What the visualization shows:
This plot displays the sampling distribution of proportions under the null hypothesis. The shaded region to the left of the observed sample proportion represents the lower-tail p-value—the probability of obtaining a sample proportion as small or smaller than the observed value if the true proportion is 0.30. This helps assess whether there is evidence that the true population proportion is less than the hypothesized value.
Dear Data Postcard
Title: A Week of Music: Necklace Visualization











Dataset and variables:
This hand-drawn visualization represents a week of self-recorded music listening data. Each “necklace” corresponds to a day of the week, with visual encodings showing different attributes of listening behavior. Color represents mood (e.g., sad, relaxing, inspiring, happy), while shapes and patterns indicate activities (such as walking, sleeping, cleaning, working, or exercising) and duration of listening time.
What the visualization shows:
This piece transforms daily music habits into a creative, wearable-style design. It shows how listening patterns vary across days, highlighting shifts in mood, activity, and time spent listening. The necklace format emphasizes continuity over time, making the data feel personal and expressive rather than purely analytical.
Another Visualization from the Course



Title: 2D Density (Contour Heatmap) of Pulse and Height
Dataset and variables:
This visualization uses the survey dataset. The two main variables are Height, representing individuals’ heights, and Pulse, representing their pulse rates. These continuous variables are used to explore the joint distribution of height and pulse.
What the visualization shows:
This contour heatmap displays the density of observations across combinations of height and pulse. Areas with darker shading and tighter contour lines indicate regions where data points are more concentrated. The plot helps reveal where most individuals fall in terms of height and pulse, and whether any patterns or relationships exist between the two variables.

articles.title: The Architecture of Sleep
articles.description: A data visualization exhibit about sleep, daily habits, and performance.
articles.contents:
The Architecture of Sleep
Introduction
Sleep is shaped by more than just the number of hours a person spends in bed. Daily routines, work schedules, caffeine use, sleep aids, health risks, and feelings of rest all influence how well people sleep and how they perform the next day.
This exhibit uses the Sleep Health and Daily Performance Dataset from Kaggle. The dataset contains 100,000 observations and 32 variables related to sleep behavior, health, lifestyle habits, and cognitive performance. Some of the main variables in this exhibit include sleep duration, sleep quality score, work hours, caffeine before bed, sleep aid use, sleep disorder risk, and cognitive performance.
The goal of this exhibit is to tell a visual story about how modern habits shape rest. The story begins with a summary of the dataset, then moves into daily habits, stimulant use, sleep aids, health risk, and cognitive performance.
Color Palette
For this exhibit, I created a night-inspired color palette to match the theme of sleep and daily performance. The palette uses deep blue to represent nighttime and rest, soft blue and lavender to create a calm atmosphere, moonlight gray for contrast, and coral as an accent color for important trends and comparisons.
Using the same palette across the exhibit helps the visualizations feel connected as one cohesive virtual art exhibit.
Part 1: Understanding the Dataset
The first step in telling the story of sleep is understanding the overall shape of the data. Before examining habits or outcomes, it is important to look at the basic distributions of sleep duration and sleep quality.
Visualizations 1 and 2 provide this foundation. The distribution of sleep duration shows how many hours people typically sleep, while the distribution of sleep quality scores shows how participants’ sleep is rated across the dataset. Together, these graphs introduce the two central sleep variables used throughout the exhibit.
Visualization 1


ggplot(sleep, aes(x = sleep_duration_hrs)) +
  geom_histogram(
    fill = sleep_palette["soft_blue"],
    color = "white",
    bins = 30
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Sleep Duration",
    x = "Sleep Duration (hours)",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_sleep()



Title: Distribution of Sleep Duration
Dataset and variables:
This visualization uses sleep_duration_hrs, which measures the number of hours individuals slept.
What the visualization shows:
This histogram shows how sleep duration is distributed across the dataset. It helps identify the most common sleep lengths and gives viewers a baseline understanding of typical sleep patterns.
Visualization 2


ggplot(sleep, aes(x = sleep_quality_score)) +
  geom_histogram(
    fill = sleep_palette["lavender"],
    color = "white",
    bins = 30
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Sleep Quality Scores",
    x = "Sleep Quality Score",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_sleep()



Title: Distribution of Sleep Quality
Dataset and variables:
This visualization uses sleep_quality_score, which measures reported sleep quality.
What the visualization shows:
This histogram shows how sleep quality scores are distributed across individuals. It helps establish how people generally rate their sleep before the exhibit moves into possible influences on sleep quality.
Part 2: Daily Habits and Sleep Quality
After looking at daily habits and bedtime screen use, the third section focuses on behaviors that may directly affect sleep or reflect attempts to manage sleep problems. Caffeine before bed and sleep aid use are important because they represent choices people make near sleep time.
Visualization 3 examines the relationship between sleep duration and sleep quality. This graph connects the amount of sleep someone gets to the quality of that sleep.
Visualization 4 looks at work hours and sleep duration. This adds another part to the story by asking whether longer work days may be associated with shorter sleep.
Visualization 5 explores screen time before bed and sleep quality. This visualization shifts the story from daytime demands to nighttime routines, showing how behavior right before sleep may also affect rest.
Together, these visualizations suggest that sleep quality is connected not only to how long people sleep, but also to the structure of their day and the habits they carry into bedtime.
Visualization 3


ggplot(sleep, aes(x = sleep_duration_hrs, y = sleep_quality_score)) +
  geom_point(
    alpha = 0.10,
    color = sleep_palette["soft_blue"]
  ) +
  geom_smooth(
    color = sleep_palette["coral"],
    se = FALSE,
    linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Sleep Duration and Sleep Quality",
    x = "Sleep Duration (hours)",
    y = "Sleep Quality Score"
  ) +
  theme_sleep()



Title: Sleep Duration and Sleep Quality
Dataset and variables:
This visualization uses sleep_duration_hrs and sleep_quality_score.
What the visualization shows:
This plot shows whether people who sleep longer tend to report higher sleep quality. The trend line helps summarize the overall relationship between sleep quantity and sleep experience.
Visualization 4


ggplot(sleep, aes(x = work_hours_that_day, y = sleep_duration_hrs)) +
  geom_point(
    alpha = 0.08,
    color = sleep_palette["lavender"]
  ) +
  geom_smooth(
    color = sleep_palette["night_blue"],
    se = FALSE,
    linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Work Hours and Sleep Duration",
    x = "Work Hours That Day",
    y = "Sleep Duration (hours)"
  ) +
  theme_sleep()



Title: Work Hours and Sleep Duration
Dataset and variables:
This visualization uses work_hours_that_day and sleep_duration_hrs.
What the visualization shows:
This plot explores how the number of hours worked in a day relates to sleep duration. It helps show whether heavier work demands may be connected to getting less sleep.
Visualization 5


ggplot(sleep, aes(x = screen_time_before_bed_mins, y = sleep_quality_score)) +
  geom_point(
    alpha = 0.08,
    color = sleep_palette["lavender"]
  ) +
  geom_smooth(
    color = sleep_palette["coral"],
    se = FALSE,
    linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Screen Time Before Bed and Sleep Quality",
    x = "Screen Time Before Bed (minutes)",
    y = "Sleep Quality Score"
  ) +
  theme_sleep()



Title: The Glow Before Sleep
Dataset and variables:
This visualization uses screen_time_before_bed_mins and sleep_quality_score. Screen time before bed measures how many minutes an individual used a screen before sleeping, while sleep quality score measures the reported quality of sleep.
What the visualization shows:
This plot explores whether more screen time before bed is associated with lower sleep quality. It adds to the story by showing how bedtime routines, not just daytime habits, may shape the quality of rest.
Part 3: Stimulants, Sleep Aids, and Sleep Quality
The third section focuses on behaviors that may directly affect sleep or reflect attempts to manage sleep problems. Caffeine before bed and sleep aid use are important because they represent choices people make near sleep time.
Visualization 6 explores the relationship between caffeine before bed and sleep quality. Since caffeine is a stimulant, this graph helps examine whether higher caffeine intake before sleep is associated with lower sleep quality.
Visualization 7 compares sleep quality between people who used sleep aids and those who did not. This graph adds complexity because sleep aid use may not simply mean better sleep. It may also indicate that a person was already struggling with sleep.
Together, these visualizations show that sleep-related behaviors are not always simple. Some habits may interfere with rest, while others may reflect efforts to improve it.
Visualization 6


ggplot(sleep, aes(x = caffeine_mg_before_bed, y = sleep_quality_score)) +
  geom_point(
    alpha = 0.08,
    color = sleep_palette["soft_blue"]
  ) +
  geom_smooth(
    color = sleep_palette["coral"],
    se = FALSE,
    linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Caffeine Before Bed and Sleep Quality",
    x = "Caffeine Before Bed (mg)",
    y = "Sleep Quality Score"
  ) +
  theme_sleep()



Title: Caffeine and Sleep Quality
Dataset and variables:
This visualization uses caffeine_mg_before_bed and sleep_quality_score.
What the visualization shows:
This plot shows how caffeine intake before bed relates to sleep quality. It helps reveal whether higher caffeine consumption near bedtime is associated with poorer sleep.
Visualization 7


ggplot(sleep, aes(x = sleep_aid_used, y = sleep_quality_score, fill = sleep_aid_used)) +
  geom_boxplot(
    alpha = 0.85,
    color = sleep_palette["night_blue"]
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "No Sleep Aid" = sleep_palette["soft_blue"],
      "Used Sleep Aid" = sleep_palette["coral"]
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Sleep Aid Use and Sleep Quality",
    x = "Sleep Aid Use",
    y = "Sleep Quality Score"
  ) +
  theme_sleep() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")



Title: Sleep Aids and Sleep Quality
Dataset and variables:
This visualization uses sleep_aid_used and sleep_quality_score.
What the visualization shows:
This boxplot compares sleep quality between individuals who used sleep aids and those who did not. It raises the question of whether sleep aids are linked to better sleep or whether they are more common among people already experiencing sleep difficulties.
Part 4: Sleep, Health Risk, and Performance
The final section connects sleep to broader outcomes. After looking at habits and sleep-related behaviors, the exhibit ends by asking what sleep may mean for health risk and daily functioning.
Visualization 8 compares sleep quality across levels of sleep disorder risk. Visualization 9 examines the relationship between sleep duration and cognitive performance. Visualization 10 compares cognitive performance between people who felt rested and those who did not.
Together, these visualizations show that sleep is connected to both health and performance. The exhibit ends by emphasizing that sleep quality is not just about comfort; it may also reflect broader patterns in well-being and daily life.
Visualization 8


ggplot(sleep, aes(x = sleep_disorder_risk, y = sleep_quality_score, fill = sleep_disorder_risk)) +
  geom_boxplot(
    alpha = 0.85,
    color = sleep_palette["night_blue"]
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Healthy" = sleep_palette["soft_blue"],
      "Low" = sleep_palette["moon"],
      "Moderate" = sleep_palette["lavender"],
      "High" = sleep_palette["coral"]
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Sleep Disorder Risk and Sleep Quality",
    x = "Sleep Disorder Risk",
    y = "Sleep Quality Score"
  ) +
  theme_sleep() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")



Title: Sleep Disorder Risk and Sleep Quality
Dataset and variables:
This visualization uses sleep_disorder_risk and sleep_quality_score.
What the visualization shows:
This boxplot shows how sleep quality differs across levels of sleep disorder risk. It helps connect individual sleep experiences to broader health-related concerns.
Visualization 9


ggplot(sleep, aes(x = sleep_duration_hrs, y = cognitive_performance_score)) +
  stat_density_2d(
    aes(fill = after_stat(level)),
    geom = "polygon",
    color = "white"
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low = sleep_palette["moon"],
    high = sleep_palette["night_blue"]
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Sleep Duration and Cognitive Performance",
    x = "Sleep Duration (hours)",
    y = "Cognitive Performance Score",
    fill = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_sleep()



Title: Sleep and Performance
Dataset and variables:
This visualization uses sleep_duration_hrs and cognitive_performance_score.
What the visualization shows:
This density plot shows where combinations of sleep duration and cognitive performance are most concentrated. It helps explore whether certain sleep durations are associated with stronger daily performance.
Visualization 10


ggplot(sleep, aes(x = felt_rested, y = cognitive_performance_score, fill = felt_rested)) +
  geom_boxplot(
    alpha = 0.85,
    color = sleep_palette["night_blue"]
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Did Not Feel Rested" = sleep_palette["lavender"],
      "Felt Rested" = sleep_palette["coral"]
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Feeling Rested and Cognitive Performance",
    x = "Rested Status",
    y = "Cognitive Performance Score"
  ) +
  theme_sleep() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")



Title: Feeling Rested and Performance
Dataset and variables:
This visualization uses felt_rested and cognitive_performance_score.
What the visualization shows:
This boxplot compares cognitive performance between individuals who felt rested and those who did not. It brings the story back to the lived experience of sleep: not just how long someone sleeps, but whether they wake up feeling restored.
Conclusion
Together, these visualizations show that sleep is connected to many parts of daily life. The exhibit begins by summarizing sleep duration and sleep quality, then explores how work habits, caffeine, and sleep aid use relate to sleep outcomes. Finally, it connects sleep to health risk and cognitive performance.
While these visualizations do not prove causation, they reveal meaningful patterns that can guide deeper statistical analysis. Overall, the exhibit suggests that sleep is not an isolated behavior. It is shaped by daily routines, lifestyle choices, health risk, and how people function after waking.
